metadata.purpose: Localized actionable advice for Community Health Risk Radar
metadata.use_case: Rural Bangladesh community health worker guidance

advice: Reduce salt now by avoiding added table salt, packaged chips, instant noodles, and very salty pickles.
Walk briskly for at least 30 minutes on most days of the week if there is no medical restriction.
Recheck blood pressure at the next community clinic visit or within 1-3 months.
Low.community_worker_note: Explain that early control can prevent stroke, kidney disease, and heart problems.
Low.when_to_refer: Refer sooner if the patient has headache, chest pain, weakness, shortness of breath, or swelling.

advice: Cut salt immediately: cook with less salt, avoid adding extra salt at the table, and limit salty snacks and preserved foods.
Encourage daily walking or light physical work for a total of about 150 minutes per week if the person is safe to exercise.
Arrange a repeat blood pressure check within 1-2 weeks at a clinic or through a trained health worker.
Medium.community_worker_note: Tell the patient that repeated readings above the normal range need follow-up even if they feel well.
Medium.when_to_refer: Refer to a health facility if the reading stays high after rest or if symptoms of stroke or heart trouble appear.

advice: Arrange urgent clinic review the same day or within 24 hours if possible, especially if the blood pressure remains very high after rest.
Advise strict salt reduction right away and avoidance of energy drinks, tobacco, and heavy exertion until reviewed by a clinician.
Teach danger signs: severe headache, blurred vision, chest pain, one-sided weakness, or trouble speaking need emergency referral.
High.community_worker_note: Do not delay referral if the patient has very high readings or any danger sign.
High.when_to_refer: Immediate referral to a higher-level facility or emergency care if danger signs are present.

advice: Reduce sugary drinks and sweets, and choose water, unsweetened tea, or plain milk instead.
Keep meal portions steady and include vegetables, lentils, fish, eggs, and other protein foods where available.
Repeat diabetes screening at routine community follow-up if risk factors increase.
Low.community_worker_note: Explain early warning signs such as frequent urination, increased thirst, fatigue, and unexplained weight loss.
Low.when_to_refer: Refer for glucose testing if symptoms appear or if there is a strong family history.

advice: Ask the patient to stop sugary drinks, reduce white rice portion size, and use more vegetables, lentils, and protein at meals.
Encourage a 20-30 minute walk after meals on most days if safe and culturally acceptable.
Arrange a fasting blood glucose or HbA1c test at the nearest clinic within 1-2 weeks.
Medium.community_worker_note: Tell the patient that diabetes can begin quietly, so screening is important even without obvious symptoms.
Medium.when_to_refer: Refer sooner if there is weight loss, blurred vision, slow-healing wounds, or repeated infections.

advice: Refer for prompt clinical assessment and glucose testing as soon as possible, ideally the same day.
Advise immediate reduction of sugar, sweet tea, and sugary snacks while waiting for clinical review.
Teach urgent warning signs: vomiting, drowsiness, deep or fast breathing, severe dehydration, or confusion require emergency referral.
High.community_worker_note: Explain that high sugar can become dangerous quickly and needs formal treatment planning.
High.when_to_refer: Immediate referral to a health facility or emergency care if severe symptoms are present.

advice: Improve meal diversity by adding eggs, fish, dal, milk, leafy vegetables, and seasonal fruit when available.
Encourage regular meals and snacks instead of long gaps between eating.
Monitor weight and appetite at the next community follow-up visit.
Low.community_worker_note: For children, pregnant women, and older adults, remind families that early nutrition support prevents further decline.
Low.when_to_refer: Refer if the person keeps losing weight, has poor appetite, or has swelling.

advice: Increase energy and protein intake with extra rice or bread plus dal, fish, egg, or milk at each main meal.
For pregnant or breastfeeding women, encourage one extra meal or healthy snack each day and early antenatal follow-up.
Check weight, appetite, and illness history again within 1-2 weeks.
Medium.community_worker_note: Explain that repeated fever, diarrhea, or poor food access can worsen malnutrition quickly.
Medium.when_to_refer: Refer if the person is not improving, has very low body weight, or has signs of infection.

advice: Refer urgently for nutritional assessment and medical review, especially for children, pregnant women, older adults, or anyone with visible wasting.
Advise immediate small frequent meals, oral rehydration if diarrhea is present, and family support for food access.
Check for danger signs such as severe weakness, dehydration, swelling, fever, or inability to eat.
High.community_worker_note: High-risk malnutrition needs quick referral because infections and dehydration can become life-threatening.
High.when_to_refer: Immediate referral to a clinic or hospital if danger signs, severe weight loss, or inability to eat are present.
